The court also addressed Section 3(2) of the Land Registration Ordinance, which governs how leasehold interests must be registered and prioritised at the Land Registry. The Milmo principle had been applied in Hong Kong for eight decades after its importation from English common law.
items.whatItMeans: A sub-tenancy is a lease granted by an existing tenant to a third party, creating a chain: property owner grants head lease to tenant A, tenant A grants a sub-lease to sub-tenant B. Under the abolished Milmo principle, if the sub-lease ran longer than the head lease, sub-tenant B automatically became the direct tenant of the property owner by operation of law, regardless of what the parties intended. The CFA's ruling ends this automatic conversion.
clients: Landlords, developers, and tenants in Hong Kong who have granted or received sub-tenancies where the sub-lease term exceeds the remaining duration of the head lease should seek immediate legal advice on how the abolition of the Milmo principle changes the legal character of those arrangements and their implications at the Land Registry.

Banks and lenders holding security over leasehold property in Hong Kong should review their security packages for overlong sub-lease arrangements previously classified as assignments under the Milmo principle, since reclassification could affect the nature and enforceability of the security interest they took.

Parties to commercial property disputes involving overlong sub-leases created before 17 April 2026 should reassess their legal position, since the CFA's ruling may invalidate assumptions previously made about whether certain interests took effect as sub-leases or as assignments.
lawFirms: Real estate practices must update standard lease drafting checklists to require verification that any sub-lease term does not exceed the remaining life of the head lease, and must advise clients that the Milmo conversion safety valve no longer exists if parties inadvertently create an overlong sub-tenancy.

Litigation teams handling landlord-tenant disputes should assess whether arguments previously based on the Milmo automatic conversion now need to be reframed, and should consider whether the CFA's analysis of Section 3(2) of the Land Registration Ordinance gives rise to new claims about historical registrations.

Conveyancing and property finance counsel should update title investigation procedures and registration opinion letters to reflect the changed analysis of overlong sub-leases under Section 3(2), as set out in the CFA judgment.

sources.title: Court of Final Appeal gives landmark judgment abolishing the Milmo principle (Denis Chang's Chambers)
CFA ends long-standing land law principle (Boase Cohen and Collins)

items.headline: Hong Kong stocks jump 1.7% as AI and chip names lead a tech charge
items.whatsTheNews: Hong Kong's benchmark Hang Seng Index rose 1.72 per cent, or 446 points, to close at 26,394 on 16 April 2026, driven by a sharp rally in technology shares. The Hang Seng Tech Index climbed 3.67 per cent to 5,092 points. Battery maker CATL gained about 9 per cent, search giant Baidu rose about 7 per cent and Alibaba added about 5 per cent, while chipmaker SMIC jumped around 10 per cent. Mainland markets also rose, with the Shanghai Composite up 0.7 per cent and the Shenzhen Component up 2.05 per cent. The move reflected growing investor confidence in China's semiconductor and AI sectors as demand for AI hardware kept building.
items.whatItMeans: The Hang Seng Index is the main scoreboard for the Hong Kong stock market, tracking its biggest listed companies; when it rises, it broadly means investors are feeling more optimistic. The Hang Seng Tech Index is a sub-index focused on big technology names, so a 3.7 per cent jump there signals money rushing specifically into tech. A lot of 2026's market story in Hong Kong is about artificial intelligence: investors are betting that Chinese chipmakers (like SMIC) and AI-linked platforms (like Baidu and Alibaba) will profit from the global AI boom. Days like this matter because the strength of the secondary market, where existing shares trade, feeds directly into the IPO market, where new companies list. A buoyant market makes it easier and more attractive for companies to come to Hong Kong to raise money.
clients: Listed technology companies benefit when their share prices climb, as a higher valuation makes it cheaper to raise fresh capital and gives them stronger currency for acquisitions.
Companies considering an IPO read rallies like this as a green light, since strong investor appetite for tech and AI names improves the odds of a successful, well-priced listing.
Asset managers and retail investors holding Hong Kong tech see gains on paper, but the concentration in a handful of AI and chip names also raises the risk of a sharp pullback if sentiment turns.
lawFirms: Capital markets practices tend to get busier when markets run hot, because confident companies launch IPOs, follow-on share sales and convertible bonds that all need legal structuring.
Funds and asset-management lawyers advise the institutions piling into Chinese tech, handling mandates, disclosure and the regulatory side of building these positions.
Litigation and regulatory teams keep an eye on rallies concentrated in a few stocks, as rapid price swings can later trigger market-misconduct questions and shareholder disputes.

sources.title: Hang Seng reaches 26,394 points as tech stocks rally (The Standard)

items.headline: Hong Kong's exchange courts tech founders with looser dual-class rules as consultation closes
items.whatsTheNews: The consultation on HKEX's Listing Framework Competitiveness Review closed on 8 May 2026, after the exchange published its proposals on 13 March. This is described as the biggest overhaul of Hong Kong's listing rules since the 2018 reforms that first allowed weighted voting rights and biotech listings. The exchange proposes letting very large companies with a market value of at least HK$40 billion use a weighted voting rights cap of up to 20 to 1, double the current 10 to 1. It also proposes cutting the market value thresholds that let companies use dual-class structures, and letting all applicants file their listing applications confidentially. HKEX is the operator of the Hong Kong stock exchange and writes the listing rules that companies must follow.
items.whatItMeans: A listing framework is the set of rules a company must meet to sell its shares to the public on an exchange. Weighted voting rights, also called dual-class shares, let founders keep more votes per share than ordinary investors, so they keep control even after selling a big stake. A cap of 20 to 1 means a founder's share can carry up to twenty times the votes of a normal share. Confidential filing lets a company submit its listing application privately and only go public later, which protects it if it decides not to proceed. A consultation is the formal process where an exchange asks the market for comments before changing the rules, and the closing date is the deadline for those comments.
clients: Technology and innovative founders gain a stronger reason to list in Hong Kong, because looser dual-class rules let them raise money without losing control. Lower thresholds open the door to more mid-sized companies that were previously shut out.

Companies weighing where to list now see Hong Kong competing harder with New York and London on founder-friendly terms and confidential filing. That choice affects valuations, the investor base and how much they can raise.

Investors face a trade-off, because dual-class structures concentrate power in founders and can reduce the say of ordinary shareholders. They will price that governance risk into how much they pay for these shares.
lawFirms: Capital markets teams will be busy responding to the consultation for clients and then advising issuers on how to structure listings under the new rules. Candidates should care because rule changes create a wave of fresh IPO mandates.

Corporate and M&A teams advise founders on share structures, control arrangements and the governance terms that come with weighted voting rights. This is exactly where company law strategy meets deal-making.

Equity capital markets lawyers acting for sponsors and banks will need to master the new thresholds and confidential filing mechanics quickly. A trainee who tracks these reforms becomes valuable on every listing team in the city.

sources.title: HKEX Launches Competitiveness Review of Listing Framework
Exchange Publishes Consultation Paper on Proposals to Enhance Listing Competitiveness

items.headline: Electronics giant Huaqin launches a US$580 million Hong Kong listing with JPMorgan and UBS on board
items.whatsTheNews: Huaqin Technology, a Shanghai-listed contract manufacturer that builds smartphones, laptops and servers for big brands, kicked off a Hong Kong share sale on 15 April 2026 aiming to raise about HK$4.55 billion (US$580 million). It offered 58.55 million shares at up to HK$77.70 each, a 31 per cent discount to its mainland share price. JPMorgan and UBS managed the deal. Seventeen cornerstone investors, including JPMorgan Asset Management, UBS Asset Management and Taikang Life, committed about US$290 million, taking roughly half the shares on offer. The final price was due by 22 April and trading was set to start on 23 April. Huaqin is the world's largest consumer-electronics ODM by shipments.
items.whatItMeans: Huaqin is doing an 'A+H' listing, meaning its shares already trade on a mainland Chinese exchange (the A-share market) and it is now adding a Hong Kong listing (the H-share market) to reach international investors. Cornerstone investors are large funds that agree in advance to buy a big chunk of an IPO and hold for a set lock-up period, which signals confidence and helps the deal get done. An ODM (original design manufacturer) designs and builds products that other companies sell under their own brand. The fact that blue-chip names like JPMorgan and UBS are both running and buying into the deal shows Hong Kong's IPO market is hot in 2026 and that global money still wants exposure to Chinese tech-supply-chain companies. The discount to the mainland price is normal, as Hong Kong-listed shares of dual-listed firms usually trade cheaper.
clients: Mainland Chinese companies already listed at home are watching deals like this to gauge whether a second Hong Kong listing can pull in international capital, and Huaqin's strong cornerstone demand is an encouraging signal for the pipeline.
Institutional investors get another large, liquid tech-supply-chain name to buy, and the heavy cornerstone take-up shows big funds are competing hard for allocations in Hong Kong's busy IPO market.

Global banks such as JPMorgan and UBS benefit directly, earning fees from underwriting and advisory roles, and a high-profile mandate like this strengthens their pitch for the next wave of A+H listings.
lawFirms: Equity capital markets teams are central to A+H deals, drafting the Hong Kong prospectus, negotiating cornerstone agreements and coordinating with mainland counsel, so a steady flow of these listings means steady, high-value work.
Lawyers advising cornerstone investors negotiate lock-up terms and review disclosure, and the 17-investor line-up here shows how much of this specialised, deadline-driven work each big IPO generates.
Firms with both Hong Kong and mainland China capability are best placed to win A+H mandates, because these deals require navigating two regulators and two sets of listing rules at once.

sources.title: Huaqin's US$580 million Hong Kong share sale attracts JPMorgan and UBS (SCMP)
China's Huaqin seeks to raise nearly $581 million in HK listing amid market volatility (Reuters)

items.headline: Beijing lines up its biggest Hong Kong yuan bond sale since 2023 to push the renminbi abroad
items.whatsTheNews: China's Ministry of Finance said on 15 April 2026 that it would sell about 15.5 billion yuan (US$2.3 billion) of offshore yuan-denominated government bonds in Hong Kong, with the sale scheduled for 22 April. The notes span two, three, five and 15-year maturities. It was set to be China's largest such issuance in Hong Kong since 2023 and its second offshore sale of the year, topping a 14-billion-yuan offering in February. The deal landed as investors showed growing appetite for yuan-backed assets amid geopolitical uncertainty. These instruments, known as dim sum bonds, are issued outside mainland China but priced in yuan, deepening the pool of yuan assets available in Hong Kong.
items.whatItMeans: A bond is an IOU: an investor lends money and gets regular interest plus their money back at the end. Government bonds are issued by a state, here China. 'Offshore yuan' or 'dim sum' bonds are yuan-denominated bonds sold outside mainland China, mostly in Hong Kong, so foreign investors can hold yuan assets without dealing directly with mainland markets. Beijing's long-term goal is 'renminbi internationalisation', getting the yuan used more widely around the world for trade and investment so China relies less on the US dollar. Hong Kong is the main testing ground for this, because it is a global financial centre inside China's borders. Each large issuance gives international investors more yuan products to buy and builds the city's role as the offshore yuan hub. The record-low borrowing costs China achieved also show strong demand.
clients: International investors and funds get a large, safe yuan asset to add to their portfolios, useful for anyone wanting exposure to the Chinese currency without trading on mainland exchanges.
Banks in Hong Kong that handle offshore yuan business gain from the deeper, more active market, as more issuance means more trading, custody and treasury activity flowing through the city.
Chinese and multinational companies that want to raise yuan funding benefit when sovereign deals like this set pricing benchmarks and show there is strong demand for offshore yuan paper.
lawFirms: Debt capital markets teams draft the offering documents and advise issuers and underwriters on these sovereign bond sales, and a record-size deal means high-profile, complex work.
Banking and finance lawyers advising on offshore yuan products benefit as the market deepens, since more issuance brings more structuring, regulatory and cross-border questions.
Firms positioning around renminbi internationalisation see sovereign issuances as anchor deals that build Hong Kong's offshore yuan ecosystem and generate follow-on corporate mandates.

sources.title: China set to issue US$2.3b in offshore bonds as demand for yuan-backed assets grows (SCMP)

items.headline: China's First 'Spatial Intelligence' Firm Launches a Hot Hong Kong IPO
items.whatsTheNews: In mid April 2026 Manycore Tech, a Hangzhou based software company, ran a closely watched Hong Kong IPO and listed on the main board on 17 April under stock code 00068. The company priced its shares at the top of the range at HK$7.62 and raised around HK$1.2 billion. The Hong Kong public portion was massively oversubscribed, by about 1,591 times, and cornerstone investors including major insurers committed around HK$455 million. On its first trading day the stock soared, closing roughly 144 percent above the offer price. Manycore became the first of Hangzhou's so called Six Little Dragons, a group of buzzy tech startups, to reach the public market, and it pitches itself as the world's first listed spatial intelligence company.
items.whatItMeans: Spatial intelligence means AI that can understand and operate in the physical, three dimensional world, used in things like design software, robots and self driving vehicles, rather than just handling text. Oversubscribed means investors asked for far more shares than were on offer, here roughly 1,591 times more, which signals red hot demand. Cornerstone investors are large institutions that agree to buy a big block of shares before the IPO and hold them for a set period, which reassures other buyers. Pricing at the top of the range means the company sold shares at the highest price it had advertised. The frenzy shows how much appetite there is for Chinese technology listings in Hong Kong right now, and why the city is leading global IPO tables.
clients: Mainland technology startups watching this debut will be encouraged to choose Hong Kong for their own listings, especially the other Six Little Dragons, because the strong demand and high first day pop suggest they can raise money on good terms. Success stories pull more of the pipeline toward Hong Kong.

Cornerstone and retail investors who got in benefited from the surge, but the huge first day jump also raises questions about pricing and volatility that boards and underwriters must manage. Future issuers will study how Manycore balanced demand against a sustainable share price.

Suppliers, banks and advisers tied to the tech sector gain from a healthier listing market because newly cash rich companies spend on expansion, talent and acquisitions. A buoyant IPO for a marquee name lifts sentiment across the ecosystem.
lawFirms: Capital markets teams run these technology IPOs, handling the prospectus, the listing application and cornerstone agreements, and a name brand deal like this is exactly the experience juniors want on their CV. The volume of tech listings means real, repeatable work.

China and cross border lawyers structure how a mainland company lists in Hong Kong, dealing with two regulatory systems and the rules for overseas listings by Chinese issuers. A candidate who understands that bridge will be in demand.

Because the stock jumped so sharply on day one, regulatory and disputes teams may field questions about pricing, allocation and disclosure, reminding trainees that a successful float still carries follow on legal risk. Getting the disclosure right protects everyone involved.

sources.title: Manycore Tech announces Hong Kong IPO
Manycore, the first of the Hangzhou 'Little Dragons' to go public, pushes 'spatial intelligence' as the next wave of AI development

items.headline: HKEX censures a new listing for hiding RMB160 million of loans in its IPO paperwork
items.whatsTheNews: Hong Kong's stock exchange operator publicly censured Cloud Factory Technology Holdings (stock code 2512) and four of its directors on 14 April 2026. The exchange found that the company's IPO prospectus and a related financial forecast contained material discrepancies with its actual finances. Specifically, the documents failed to disclose planned or executed bank loans and capital spending that each exceeded RMB160 million, information the exchange called material to investors. The directors knew about these transactions but did not tell the IPO sponsor or make sure they were reflected in the listing documents. The exchange ordered the company to hire a compliance adviser and made each director attend training.
items.whatItMeans: When a company lists on the stock exchange (an IPO, or initial public offering), it publishes a prospectus, a long legal document that tells investors everything material about the business so they can decide whether to buy the shares. A sponsor is the investment bank that vets the company and stands behind that document. The law requires the prospectus to be accurate and complete, because investors rely on it. Here the directors left out big loans and spending, so investors got a misleading picture of the company's debt. The exchange polices its own rulebook through disciplinary action: it can censure (a formal public telling-off), force remedial steps, and name and shame directors. This case is a reminder that listing rules are not a box-ticking exercise, and that directors carry personal responsibility for what goes into the prospectus.
clients: Companies preparing to list in Hong Kong now have a fresh warning that the exchange checks prospectus figures against reality and will act when they do not match. Boards will lean harder on advisers to make sure every material loan, contract and spending plan is captured before the document goes out.

Directors of listed and listing companies face personal exposure here, not just the company. The censure and mandatory training show that 'I left it to the finance team' is not a defence, which pushes directors to ask sharper questions during the IPO process.

Investment banks acting as sponsors will treat this as a prompt to dig deeper into a client's financials, because a sponsor that misses red flags like undisclosed loans can itself face regulatory trouble and reputational damage.
lawFirms: Capital markets teams advising on IPOs will use this case to press clients on full disclosure and to document the due diligence trail, since the exchange clearly compares the prospectus against the company's real numbers after listing.

Corporate governance and contentious regulatory lawyers can expect more instructions defending directors in exchange disciplinary proceedings, and will advise boards on verification processes that reduce the risk of a censure.

Firms running director training programmes have a live example to teach from, as the exchange itself ordered training here, underlining that ongoing education is now part of how regulators expect boards to behave.

sources.title: Exchange's Disciplinary Action against Cloud Factory Technology Holdings Limited (Stock Code: 2512) and Four Directors (HKEX)
Key Regulatory Updates for Hong Kong Listed Companies — March/April 2026 (Latham & Watkins)

items.headline: HKMA Reports Exchange Fund Foreign Assets Fell HK$59.5 Billion in March Amid Global Market Turbulence
items.whatsTheNews: The Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) published the analytical accounts of the Exchange Fund on 14 April 2026, showing that foreign assets fell by HK$59.5 billion during March 2026 to HK$3,570.6 billion (approximately US$460 billion). The Exchange Fund is the pool of assets managed by the HKMA to underpin Hong Kong's Linked Exchange Rate System (LERS), which has pegged the Hong Kong dollar to the US dollar at a central rate of HK$7.80 within a permitted trading band of HK$7.75 to HK$7.85 since 1983. The March decline occurred during a period of global financial market turbulence linked to US tariff announcements, which depressed the market value of assets in the Fund's investment portfolio. The Monetary Base (which includes certificates of indebtedness, government-issued notes and coins in circulation, bank settlement balances, and Exchange Fund Bills and Notes outstanding) stood at HK$2,065.0 billion. The data is released monthly under the IMF's Special Data Dissemination Standard, which requires countries with significant financial systems to publish regular, transparent data on their central bank's reserve position.
items.whatItMeans: The Exchange Fund's foreign assets are the overseas investments (primarily US government bonds and other safe, liquid assets) that back the Hong Kong dollar in circulation and give the HKMA the financial capacity to buy Hong Kong dollars in the foreign exchange market to prevent the currency from falling through its HK$7.85 floor. A fall in foreign assets typically reflects investment losses, currency defence operations, or outflows from the monetary base.
clients: Banks operating in Hong Kong monitor Exchange Fund data closely because changes in the Monetary Base affect the supply of Hong Kong dollar liquidity in the interbank market, with knock-on effects on short-term borrowing rates and the pricing of Hong Kong dollar credit facilities for corporate and retail customers.

Corporate treasury teams managing Hong Kong dollar cash positions or debt should understand that the LERS means Hong Kong dollar interest rates track US Federal Reserve decisions very closely, so any shift in US monetary policy feeds rapidly into Hong Kong dollar borrowing costs.

International investors holding Hong Kong dollar assets should track monthly Exchange Fund data as the clearest public signal of the HKMA's reserve position and its capacity to maintain the currency peg under market stress.
lawFirms: Regulatory counsel advising banks on liquidity coverage ratios should monitor Exchange Fund releases because movements in the Monetary Base affect HIBOR (the Hong Kong Interbank Offered Rate), which is embedded as a floating rate benchmark in many loan agreements, derivative contracts, and bond indentures across the territory.

Litigation teams dealing with financial disputes involving HIBOR-linked instruments should track Exchange Fund data, since unexpected tightening or easing of Hong Kong dollar liquidity can trigger commercial disputes about interest calculations or the activation of market disruption provisions.

Structured finance counsel drafting Hong Kong dollar instruments with variable-rate components should ensure documents include adequate fallback provisions for scenarios where Exchange Fund operations create unusual HIBOR market conditions, given the global shift away from interbank offered rates.

sources.title: Analytical Accounts of the Exchange Fund (end-March 2026) (HKMA, 14 April 2026)
Analytical Accounts of Exchange Fund (14 April 2026) (GovHK)

items.headline: HSBC and a Standard Chartered-led venture get Hong Kong's first stablecoin licences
items.whatsTheNews: The Hong Kong Monetary Authority has granted the city's first stablecoin issuer licences to HSBC and to Anchorpoint Financial, a joint venture of Standard Chartered's Hong Kong bank, HKT and Animoca Brands. The licences were issued under the Stablecoins Ordinance, the regime that took effect on 1 August 2025 and which drew 36 applications by the end of September 2025. HSBC plans to launch a Hong Kong dollar stablecoin in the second half of 2026, fully backed by high-quality liquid assets and supporting payments through its app and PayMe. Anchorpoint plans a phased start with institutional users. The HKMA is Hong Kong's central banking body, which now supervises stablecoin issuers while the SFC oversees crypto trading platforms.
items.whatItMeans: A stablecoin is a digital token designed to hold a steady value by being pegged to a regular currency such as the Hong Kong dollar, and backed one-for-one by safe assets the issuer holds in reserve. Unlike volatile crypto like Bitcoin, a well-run stablecoin should always be worth about one dollar, which makes it useful for payments and settlement. The Stablecoins Ordinance is Hong Kong's law requiring anyone who issues such a token to get a licence and meet strict rules on reserves and risk. The HKMA is the regulator that grants and polices these licences. High-quality liquid assets are safe, easy-to-sell holdings like cash and short-term government debt that ensure holders can always redeem their tokens at face value.
clients: Banks and payment firms that win licences can offer faster, cheaper digital payments and settlement, opening a new product line and defending against pure crypto rivals. Being first gives HSBC and the Standard Chartered venture a head start in shaping how the market works.

Merchants and businesses that adopt licensed stablecoins could cut transaction costs and settle payments around the clock, which helps cash flow. Knowing the issuer is regulated reduces the risk of holding the tokens.

Crypto and Web3 companies see Hong Kong building a credible regulated path for digital money, which attracts investment and talent to the city. The clear rules also weed out weaker players who cannot meet the reserve and licensing standards.
lawFirms: FinTech and financial regulatory teams advise applicants on licence applications, reserve rules and compliance under the Stablecoins Ordinance. Candidates should care because this is a brand-new regulated market generating fresh, specialist work.
Banking teams help issuers structure the reserve assets and the payment systems that sit behind a stablecoin. This blends traditional banking law with cutting-edge digital products.

Disputes and enforcement lawyers will eventually advise on what happens when a stablecoin issuer breaches its licence conditions or a token loses its peg. A trainee who learns this regime early gains an edge as the market grows.

sources.title: Hong Kong awards stablecoin licences to HSBC, StanChart-led group in long-awaited roll-out
HSBC and Standard Chartered-led group land Hong Kong's first stablecoin licenses

items.headline: HKMA Hands Out Hong Kong's First Stablecoin Licences to HSBC and a Standard Chartered Venture
items.whatsTheNews: On 10 April 2026, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority granted the city's first ever stablecoin issuer licences. They went to HSBC and to Anchorpoint Financial, a joint venture backed by Standard Chartered, the telecoms group HKT, and Animoca Brands. The two were chosen from a large pool of applicants under rules that took effect in 2025. Both plan to launch their Hong Kong dollar stablecoins later in 2026.
items.whatItMeans: A stablecoin is digital money designed to always equal a real currency, here one coin for one Hong Kong dollar. An issuer is the company that creates and stands behind the coin, and it must hold enough safe assets to repay every holder. A licence is official permission to do this, and the HKMA granting only two shows how high the bar is. HSBC has said it wants to plug its stablecoin into everyday tools like its banking app and PayMe.
clients: Winning a licence is just the start. The two issuers now need ongoing legal and compliance advice to launch and run their coins within the rules.
Losing applicants need a new plan. Firms that did not get a licence must decide whether to reapply, partner with a winner, or change their model, and they will seek advice on the options.
Businesses can start using stablecoins. Once these coins launch, companies will want to know the legal and tax treatment of using them for payments, which creates fresh questions for advisers.
lawFirms: A brand-new market means brand-new work. Helping issuers launch, and helping businesses use stablecoins, is advisory work that did not exist before.
Banking, payments, and crypto skills combine. The work rewards firms that can blend financial regulation with technology know-how.
Early movers build the reputation. Firms involved in the first licences become the names clients think of, which pulls in the next wave of digital-money work for juniors to cut their teeth on.

sources.title: HKMA - Granting of stablecoin issuer licences
HSBC welcomes HKMA's grant of a Hong Kong stablecoin issuer licence
HSBC, Anchorpoint granted stablecoin issuer licences - RTHK

items.headline: Hong Kong's privacy watchdog warns that AI agents are a whole new risk to your data
items.whatsTheNews: In mid March 2026 Hong Kong's Privacy Commissioner for Personal Data (PCPD) issued a statement on the security risks of agentic AI, and law firms analysed it through April 2026. Agentic AI means AI tools that act on their own to complete tasks, rather than just answering a single question, which often means giving them broad access to systems, user logins and data. The PCPD called this a whole new risk category, because that wide access raises the chance of unauthorised access, data breaches and accidental data loss. It pointed to real vulnerabilities in open source agent tools, including security flaws and a meaningful share of add ons containing malware. The PCPD recommended limiting permissions, using only trusted plug ins, isolating systems, and keeping a human in the loop for higher risk decisions.
items.whatItMeans: Agentic AI is software that can take a goal and then carry out a series of steps by itself, for example logging into accounts, moving data or making bookings, instead of waiting for a person at each step. To do that it usually needs lots of access to systems and credentials, which is exactly what makes it risky. A data breach is when personal information is exposed or stolen, and malware is malicious software hidden inside an app or plug in. Human in the loop means a person must approve the riskiest actions before the AI carries them out. The PCPD is the body that enforces Hong Kong's personal data privacy law.
clients: Companies rushing to deploy AI agents now have a clear regulator signal that they must lock down permissions and vet the tools they use, or risk breaching privacy law. This forces them to slow down and build proper controls first.

Technology vendors selling AI agents face pressure to prove their products are secure and that their plug in marketplaces are clean, because buyers will demand it. Weak security becomes a commercial dealbreaker.

Any business handling customer data, from banks to retailers, must reassess whether letting an AI agent touch that data is safe, since a breach brings legal exposure and reputational damage. The guidance gives them a checklist to work from.
lawFirms: Data protection and TMT teams will advise clients on AI governance policies, vendor contracts and breach response plans that reflect the PCPD's expectations. AI risk advice is one of the fastest growing areas of demand.

Cybersecurity and technology lawyers help draft the contractual safeguards and liability terms between companies and AI vendors, plus respond if an agent causes a leak. Candidates should care because AI is reshaping how privacy and tech law is practised.

A trainee in a TMT or data seat can expect to research how the PCPD guidance compares with overseas AI rules and help build client facing AI policies, practical exposure to a topic that will dominate the next decade of tech law.

sources.title: Agentic AI security risks: Hong Kong PCPD's guidance and other emerging regulatory responses internationally - Freshfields

items.headline: Hangzhou's Manycore opens its books for a HK$1.2 billion Hong Kong IPO
items.whatsTheNews: On 10 April 2026, Caixin reported that Manycore Tech, which runs an AI-powered spatial-design software platform, had launched the public offering for its Hong Kong listing. The company planned to issue about 161 million shares at a maximum price of HK$7.62 each, aiming to raise up to HK$1.23 billion, with trading set to begin on 17 April under the ticker 0068. The retail subscription ran from 9 to 14 April, and cornerstone investors including Taikang Life, Sunshine Life and Mirae Asset took roughly 37 percent of the offer. Manycore is the first of Hangzhou's 'Six Little Dragons', a celebrated group of fast-rising tech start-ups, to go public. The same Caixin brief noted unrelated news that ByteDance's Douyin had referred more than 50 staff to authorities over suspected bribery and fraud.
items.whatItMeans: Launching an IPO means the company officially opens its subscription period, inviting retail and institutional investors to apply for shares before they start trading. The 'maximum price' is the top of the range investors can be charged; strong demand usually pushes the final price to that ceiling. Manycore sells software that uses artificial intelligence to design and visualise physical spaces, and it brands this as 'spatial intelligence', AI that understands the real world rather than just text. Being the first of Hangzhou's 'Six Little Dragons' to list matters because it tests whether public investors will pay up for China's buzzy AI start-ups, and a strong debut tends to pull the rest of the pack toward the market.
clients: For Manycore, the listing raises cash to fund AI development and gives early venture-capital backers a way to cash out, while crowning it as the first of a famous start-up cohort to reach the public market.

For the cornerstone investors such as Taikang Life and Mirae Asset, taking 37 percent of the deal locks in a stake in a hyped AI name, but their shares are typically subject to a lock-up period during which they cannot sell, so they are betting on the long-term story.

For other Chinese AI start-ups, especially Hangzhou's remaining 'Little Dragons', Manycore's reception is a real-time gauge of investor appetite that will shape their own decisions on when and where to list.
lawFirms: Capital-markets teams handle the prospectus, due diligence and Listing Rule compliance for AI companies, which can be tricky to value and describe because much of their worth sits in software and data rather than physical assets.
Lawyers structure the offshore holding vehicle and navigate China's tightening rules on domestic firms listing through overseas entities, an area under fresh regulatory scrutiny in 2026.
A steady flow of marquee tech IPOs lets Greater China ECM practices build reputation and deal-count, helping them win the next wave of 'Little Dragons' and other AI mandates.

sources.title: Tech Brief (April 10): Manycore Tech Launches Hong Kong IPO - Caixin Global

items.headline: Hong Kong Smashes IPO Records With HK$110 Billion in Three Months
items.whatsTheNews: Hong Kong raised about HK$109.9 billion from 40 new share listings in the first three months of 2026, its best first quarter in five years. That was roughly six times the amount raised in the same period of 2025. Most of the money came from "A+H" listings, where mainland Chinese companies already listed at home add a Hong Kong listing, which made up 60% of the total. Specialist technology companies listing under a newer set of rules (Chapter 18C) added another HK$19.5 billion.
items.whatItMeans: An IPO is when a company sells its shares to the public for the first time and joins a stock exchange. An A+H listing is when a company already listed in mainland China (the "A" shares) also lists in Hong Kong (the "H" shares) to reach international investors. Chapter 18C is a special part of Hong Kong's rulebook that lets young, fast-growing tech companies list even if they are not yet profitable. A strong IPO quarter means a lot of high-value legal work flowing into the city.
clients: Mainland companies want a Hong Kong listing. It opens access to global investors and a freely usable currency, so more of them are lining up to list and need cross-border legal help.
Tech companies have a route to market. The Chapter 18C rules let promising but loss-making tech firms raise money, so founders and investors seek advice on whether they qualify.
Accuracy is everything in a listing. The paperwork and disclosure are huge, and one error can derail a deal, so companies rely heavily on their advisers.
lawFirms: IPOs are among a firm's biggest earners. Each listing means months of work and large teams, so a record quarter directly boosts revenue.
Cross-border skill wins the biggest deals. Firms with strong Hong Kong and mainland China teams, plus Mandarin-speaking lawyers, land the largest A+H listings.
A full pipeline means more hiring. IPO work needs lots of junior lawyers, so a booming market is one of the clearest signals of trainee demand.

sources.title: Hong Kong IPO market raises approximately HK$110 billion in first quarter of 2026, best performance in the past five years - KPMG
Hong Kong retains global IPO crown in first quarter but faces US mega listings - SCMP

items.headline: Hong Kong's War Chest Dips to US$430.8 Billion as Reserves Update Lands
items.whatsTheNews: On 9 April 2026 the HKMA published Hong Kong's official foreign currency reserve assets as at the end of March 2026, reporting US$430.8 billion. That was down from US$439.3 billion at the end of February. Including unsettled foreign exchange contracts, the figure was about US$430.5 billion. The HKMA stressed that these reserves are still very large, equal to over five times the currency in circulation and about 38 percent of Hong Kong's broad money supply. The monthly release is a routine but closely watched health check on the city's financial buffers.
items.whatItMeans: Foreign currency reserves are the pile of overseas assets, mostly US dollars and US government bonds, that the HKMA holds to defend the Hong Kong dollar. Hong Kong runs a linked exchange rate system, which means the local dollar is pegged to trade in a tight band around 7.8 to the US dollar, and the HKMA buys or sells currency to keep it there. Big reserves are what make that promise believable, so investors trust the peg. Currency in circulation is the cash in people's wallets, and broad money supply is roughly all the money sitting in bank deposits too. A monthly dip is normal and the buffers remain huge, so this is reassurance rather than alarm, but markets watch the trend closely.
clients: Banks and exporters rely on the stability of the Hong Kong dollar peg to price contracts and manage currency risk, so healthy reserves give them confidence to keep doing business in Hong Kong dollars. If reserves looked shaky, borrowing costs and hedging bills would rise.

International investors and fund managers read these numbers as a sign of whether Hong Kong can hold its peg under pressure, which affects how much capital they are willing to park in the city. Strong buffers help keep Hong Kong attractive as a base.

Companies with cross border operations benefit from the predictability the peg provides, because a stable currency makes planning and reporting simpler. The reassurance here supports continued investment despite the month on month fall.
lawFirms: Banking and finance teams use the macro picture when advising lenders and borrowers on loan pricing and currency clauses, so understanding reserves and the peg helps lawyers give commercially sharp advice. This is context that makes a junior sound credible in front of clients.

Financial regulatory lawyers track the HKMA's role and tools because the same authority supervises banks and runs monetary policy, and its credibility underpins the whole system. Knowing how the HKMA operates is core knowledge for anyone in this space.

Disputes and restructuring teams care because currency stability affects whether borrowers can repay foreign currency debt, which feeds into defaults and workouts. A candidate who can connect a macro data release to real client risk shows commercial awareness.

sources.title: HKMA - Official Foreign Currency Reserve Assets (end-March 2026 release)

items.headline: SFC Fines Fund Manager HK$2 Million Over Staff Who Traded for Themselves
items.whatsTheNews: On 9 April 2026 the Securities and Futures Commission (the SFC, Hong Kong's markets regulator) reprimanded and fined Impression Investment Limited HK$2 million for failing to supervise its staff and control their personal trading. The SFC also banned the firm's former responsible officer, Liu Shan, from the industry for eight months. Between January 2016 and March 2021 Mr Liu made over 2,500 personal trades without the required written approval, traded the same securities on the same day as the fund he managed more than 200 times, and joined 12 IPOs through personal accounts. The conduct breached both the Fund Manager Code of Conduct and the firm's own staff dealing policy. Impression is licensed for Type 9 regulated activity, which is asset management.
items.whatItMeans: A responsible officer is a senior person the SFC approves to oversee a licensed firm's regulated work, so they carry personal accountability. The Fund Manager Code of Conduct is the SFC rulebook that tells asset managers how to behave, including strict rules on staff personal trading to stop them front running, which means buying or selling for themselves just before they trade for the fund to profit from the price move. Type 9 regulated activity is the licence category for managing money on behalf of clients. The SFC is sending a message that firms must police their own staff's trading, and that senior managers will be held responsible when controls fail, even years after the events.
clients: Asset managers and fund houses now have a fresh reminder that weak personal account dealing controls can cost real money and reputations, so many will review and tighten their staff trading policies and monitoring. Boards will want assurance that approvals and holding periods are actually enforced.

Investors in funds benefit because tougher enforcement protects them from staff secretly profiting at the fund's expense. Clients choosing a manager may increasingly ask about compliance culture as a result.

Senior managers across the industry are reminded that the responsible officer role carries personal liability, which raises the value of strong internal compliance and may make experienced compliance hires more sought after.
lawFirms: Financial regulatory and enforcement teams act for firms and individuals under SFC investigation, advising on how to respond, negotiate and settle. This case is a textbook example of the work and shows why firms need lawyers who know the SFC's procedures inside out.

Funds and asset management lawyers help managers draft and stress test the very policies that failed here, from staff dealing rules to supervision systems. A candidate interested in this area can point to how prevention work is just as valuable as defending claims.

Employment and contentious regulatory teams pick up the fallout when bans and reprimands hit individuals, including disputes over dismissal and reputation. Trainees should see how one enforcement action ripples across several practice groups.

sources.title: SFC sanctions Impression Investment Limited and its former responsible officer over staff trading activities - Timothy Loh

items.headline: Solar and battery maker Sigenergy kicks off a HK$4.4 billion Hong Kong IPO
items.whatsTheNews: On 8 April 2026, Chinese clean-energy company Sigenergy launched the public offering for its Hong Kong listing. The firm priced its shares at HK$324.20 each and offered more than 13.5 million shares, with 10 percent set aside for Hong Kong retail investors and 90 percent for big international funds. It lined up 19 cornerstone investors, including UBS Asset Management, Goldman Sachs Asset Management, BNP Paribas Asset Management and AXA, who agreed to buy large blocks in advance. The retail subscription window closed on 13 April and the shares were due to start trading on 16 April. Sigenergy makes integrated solar, battery storage and EV-charging systems, and its revenue jumped from 58 million yuan in 2023 to 9 billion yuan in 2025.
items.whatItMeans: An IPO (initial public offering) is the first time a company sells its shares to the public and lists them on a stock exchange to raise money. A 'cornerstone investor' is a large institution (like a fund manager) that commits to buying a big chunk of the shares before the offering opens, which signals confidence and helps the deal sell. The price range and number of shares are set during the 'bookbuild', when banks gauge demand from investors. Sigenergy's deal is part of a wider boom: Hong Kong raised around HK$110 billion from IPOs in the first quarter of 2026, its best showing in five years, driven by Chinese tech and clean-energy names choosing the city to tap global capital.
clients: For a clean-energy company like Sigenergy, listing in Hong Kong unlocks a deep pool of international money to fund factory expansion and research, while raising its global profile. The presence of blue-chip cornerstone investors like Goldman Sachs and UBS asset arms is a stamp of credibility that can attract customers and future fundraising.

For institutional investors, a fast-growing company whose revenue leapt from 58 million to 9 billion yuan in two years is the kind of high-growth story that draws cornerstone commitments, but they also bear the risk that the share price falls after listing if growth slows or the green-energy market cools.

For other Chinese tech and clean-energy companies weighing where to list, Sigenergy's deal is a signal that Hong Kong's market is hungry for new offerings, encouraging more of them to pick the city over mainland exchanges or New York.
lawFirms: ECM (equity capital markets) teams act as either issuer's counsel (advising the company) or underwriters' counsel (advising the banks), drafting the prospectus, running legal due diligence on the business, and making sure disclosure meets HKEX Listing Rules. A pipeline of deals like Sigenergy means steady, high-fee work for these teams.

Lawyers negotiate the cornerstone investment agreements with each of the 19 backers, a fiddly, time-pressured exercise because every fund wants slightly different terms and the deal cannot close until they are all signed.

A busy IPO market lets firms with strong Greater China capital-markets practices win mandates and justify hiring, since the same teams are often juggling several listings at once during a hot quarter.

sources.title: Sigenergy launches Hong Kong IPO - pv magazine International

items.headline: Global firms flock to Hong Kong for cheaper yuan loans as the city's RMB pool deepens
items.whatsTheNews: On 8 April 2026, the South China Morning Post reported, citing a Standard Chartered study, that more international companies are using Hong Kong to borrow in Chinese yuan because it is now markedly cheaper than borrowing in US dollars. A key driver is the Hong Kong Monetary Authority's RMB Business Facility, a tool that supplies banks with yuan funding; launched in February 2025 with a 100 billion yuan quota, it was doubled to 200 billion yuan in February 2026 after proving popular. The facility offers yuan at rates roughly 200 basis points (2 percentage points) below US benchmark rates. Standard Chartered surveyed 300 companies across 19 sectors and found that about 24 percent of firms with yuan exposure plan to increase such financing within three years. Hong Kong now holds close to 1 trillion yuan in bank deposits, giving lenders the raw material to fund these deals.
items.whatItMeans: The 'offshore yuan' market is the pool of Chinese currency held and traded outside mainland China, with Hong Kong as its biggest hub. When US interest rates are much higher than China's, borrowing in yuan instead of dollars can save a company a lot in interest, which is what '200 basis points lower' means in plain terms. The HKMA (Hong Kong's central bank) acts as a backstop, lending yuan to commercial banks through its RMB Business Facility so they have enough to on-lend to corporate borrowers. By making cheap, reliable yuan funding available, Hong Kong is cementing its role as the gateway for global companies that trade with China and want to fund their supply chains in the Chinese currency.
clients: For multinational companies that buy from or sell to China, borrowing in yuan in Hong Kong cuts interest costs and removes the need to convert currencies, reducing both expense and exchange-rate risk on cross-border trade and procurement.

For banks in Hong Kong, deeper yuan deposits and a bigger HKMA facility mean more lending opportunities and fee income, as they intermediate between the central bank's yuan supply and corporate demand for loans and bonds.

For Beijing, every global firm that funds itself in yuan advances the long-term goal of 'RMB internationalisation', spreading use of the Chinese currency in world trade and finance and reducing reliance on the US dollar.
lawFirms: Banking and finance lawyers document the loans and bond issues, drafting the facility agreements and advising on which laws govern cross-border yuan lending, an area with extra complexity because it straddles Hong Kong and mainland rules.
A shift toward yuan financing creates demand for advice on currency controls and how money can legally move in and out of mainland China, where capital flows remain tightly regulated.
Debt capital markets teams benefit as more 'dim sum bonds' (yuan-denominated bonds issued in Hong Kong) come to market, generating prospectus and listing work alongside the loan business.

sources.title: More global firms eye Hong Kong for cheaper yuan loans, bonds: StanChart - South China Morning Post

items.headline: Hong Kong's finance chief cheers a fast start to 2026, jobs and shopping included
items.whatsTheNews: In his weekly blog published on 5 April 2026, Financial Secretary Paul Chan reviewed how Hong Kong's economy performed in the first quarter of 2026. He highlighted that goods exports rose nearly 30 percent year on year in the first two months, retail sales climbed 11.8 percent (a tenth straight month of growth), and the unemployment rate eased to 3.8 percent. On markets, he noted Hong Kong led the world for IPO fundraising, with funds raised topping HK$103 billion by late March and total capital raised (including follow-on share sales) near HK$237 billion. He pointed to a pipeline of more than 500 companies waiting to list, many from AI, semiconductors and biotech, while acknowledging the Hang Seng Index was down about 2 percent for the year so far.
items.whatItMeans: The Financial Secretary is Hong Kong's top economic official, roughly its finance minister, and his blog is a semi-official readout of how the city is doing. Pulling the figures together: exports are strong, people are spending more, more people have jobs, and companies are queuing up to raise money on the stock exchange. That broad-based 'everything is going up' picture is what the government wants investors and businesses to see. The honest note is that the stock index itself slipped, showing share prices and fundraising activity do not always move together. For students, the key idea is that these data releases (GDP, retail sales, jobless rate, IPO totals) are the raw material commercial lawyers and bankers use to judge whether clients should expand, invest or hold back.
clients: Retailers, restaurants and consumer brands get encouragement from ten straight months of rising sales, supporting decisions to open stores, hire staff or expand. A 3.8 percent jobless rate means a tighter labour market, so wage and staffing costs become a bigger planning issue.

Companies planning to raise money benefit from the government spotlighting a 500-plus listing pipeline and record fundraising; it signals strong investor appetite, especially for AI, chip and biotech firms. The soft Hang Seng Index is a reminder that valuations can still be choppy.

Exporters and manufacturers can read the near-30 percent export jump as confirmation that global demand, particularly for electronics, is holding up, helping justify production and inventory commitments despite ongoing trade-policy uncertainty.
lawFirms: Banking and finance teams use upbeat macro data to advise lenders and borrowers on the timing of new loans, refinancings and expansion financing, since a growing economy with low unemployment generally supports more lending activity.

Capital markets lawyers will note the explicit signal of a deep IPO and follow-on pipeline, pointing to sustained demand for prospectus drafting, listing applications and equity raises across the AI, semiconductor and biotech sectors the FS singled out.

Employment and commercial lawyers should track the tightening labour market: low unemployment tends to spark more disputes over poaching, non-compete clauses and retention packages, alongside advisory work as clients restructure pay to hold onto scarce talent.

sources.title: The Economy in the First Quarter of 2026 - Financial Secretary's Blog

items.headline: Hong Kong climbs to the world's fifth-biggest trader as exports roar back
items.whatsTheNews: On 2 April 2026 the Hong Kong government announced that the city had become the world's fifth-largest trading entity for merchandise (physical goods) in 2025, up two places from the year before. The ranking comes from the World Trade Organization's Global Trade Outlook and Statistics report. Hong Kong's total goods trade hit US$1,585 billion, a 17.5 percent jump on 2024, and made up about 3 percent of all world trade. It now sits behind only Mainland China, the United States, Germany and the Netherlands, and ranks as the fifth-largest exporter and sixth-largest importer of goods. The momentum carried into 2026, with February exports up 24.7 percent and imports up 29.9 percent year on year.
items.whatItMeans: A 'trading entity' is just a place that buys and sells goods across borders; the WTO ranks them by the total value of those imports and exports. Hong Kong does not make much itself, so most of these goods are passing through. The city acts as a middleman, or 'entrepot', re-exporting products (mainly electronics) between Mainland China and the rest of the world. Moving up to fifth place matters because Hong Kong's whole economic pitch is that it is a reliable gateway for trade and money, even when global politics get messy and the US and China are fighting over tariffs (taxes on imported goods). Strong trade numbers feed straight into the city's banks, shipping companies, insurers and the lawyers who paper all those cross-border deals. It is also a confidence signal: if global firms keep routing goods through Hong Kong, they are betting the city stays open and well-connected.
clients: Trading houses, manufacturers and logistics groups that route goods through Hong Kong get a fresh data point to justify keeping operations in the city. Strong, rising trade volumes mean more shipping, warehousing and financing work, and reassure boards nervous about US-China tensions that Hong Kong is still a dependable hub.

Banks and trade-finance providers benefit directly, because every shipment needs financing, letters of credit and insurance. Record trade values point to a growing pool of fee-earning work, though clients will watch whether US tariff policy eventually drags on the export numbers.

Mainland Chinese companies using Hong Kong as a springboard to global markets get validation of that strategy. The city's role as a 'connector' and 'value-adder' is exactly what these firms pay for when they set up Hong Kong subsidiaries to handle international sales.
lawFirms: International trade and customs teams gain a talking point for advising clients on supply-chain structuring, tariff classification and rules of origin (the legal tests for where a product 'comes from'), all of which get more complex as goods are re-routed through Hong Kong.

Corporate and finance lawyers see knock-on deal flow: rising trade tends to bring more cross-border supply contracts, joint ventures and trade-finance facilities, all of which need drafting and negotiating. Firms with both Hong Kong and Mainland China desks are best placed to capture this.

Disputes practices should note the flip side: bigger trade volumes mean more contracts, and more contracts mean more breaches, payment fights and shipping disputes. Hong Kong's role as a trade hub reinforces its pitch as a centre for resolving those disputes through arbitration.

sources.title: Hong Kong rises to world's fifth-largest trading entity in merchandise trade - Commerce and Economic Development Bureau
HK rises to 5th largest trading entity - news.gov.hk

items.headline: Hong Kong shoppers splash out: February retail sales jump 19.3%
items.whatsTheNews: On 1 April 2026 the Census and Statistics Department reported that Hong Kong retail sales in February were provisionally worth HK$35.0 billion, up 19.3% on the same month a year earlier. After stripping out price changes, the volume of sales rose 17.5%. Online sales alone reached about HK$3 billion, up 29% year on year, and made up 8.5% of total retail. The strongest categories were electrical goods, motor vehicles and parts, and jewellery and watches. Officials stressed reading January and February together because Lunar New Year shifts between the two months, and on that basis sales rose 11.8% over the same period in 2025.
items.whatItMeans: Retail sales measure how much consumers spend in shops and online, and economists watch them as a gauge of consumer confidence and the health of the domestic economy. Provisional means the figures are an early estimate that may be revised. Year on year compares the same month in the prior year, which smooths out seasonal swings. A near 20% jump signals shoppers are spending again, which feeds through to landlords, retailers and the wider economy after several soft years.
clients: Retailers and restaurant chains see rising sales as a reason to expand, renew leases and rehire, which can flow into new store openings and supplier contracts. Sustained growth also makes them more attractive targets for investors.

Mall owners and commercial landlords benefit because stronger tenant sales support higher rents and lower vacancy, improving the value of their properties. Some leases tie rent to a percentage of tenant turnover, so spending directly lifts income.

Consumer brands and e-commerce platforms read the 29% online jump as confirmation that digital spending keeps growing, encouraging investment in logistics, payments and online storefronts in Hong Kong.
lawFirms: Real estate teams pick up leasing, acquisition and development work as confident retailers and landlords transact more space. A recovering high street tends to revive deal flow that went quiet in downturns.

Corporate and M&A teams advise on consumer sector deals, since healthier sales make retail and food and beverage businesses more financeable and more saleable. Candidates should connect strong economic data to a busier transactions pipeline.

Employment and commercial teams support retailers scaling up staff, supplier arrangements and franchise deals. Following retail data helps a trainee explain why consumer-facing clients suddenly need more legal support.

sources.title: Provisional statistics of retail sales for February 2026 (gov.hk press release)
Feb retail sales up 19.3% (news.gov.hk)

items.headline: Hong Kong misses its own stablecoin deadline as no licences are issued by end of March
items.whatsTheNews: On 1 April 2026 it emerged that Hong Kong had missed its self-imposed target to grant the first stablecoin issuer licences by the end of March. Financial Secretary Paul Chan had said in February that licensing would begin in March, part of the city's plan to become a regulated hub for stablecoins. The HKMA had received 36 applications under the Stablecoins Ordinance but had not approved any by the deadline, and gave no public explanation or new timeline at that point. HSBC and a Standard Chartered led venture were widely expected to be among the first recipients. The delay raised questions about how fast Hong Kong's new framework would move from rules to real products.
items.whatItMeans: A stablecoin is a digital token designed to hold a steady value, usually pegged to a currency like the Hong Kong dollar, and used for payments and settling crypto trades. The Stablecoins Ordinance is Hong Kong's law requiring issuers to be licensed by the HKMA and to fully back the tokens with safe reserves. An issuer licence is official permission to create and sell a stablecoin. Missing the deadline matters because Hong Kong is competing with other financial centres to attract this business, and delays can dent confidence even if they reflect caution rather than a change of policy.
clients: Banks and fintech ventures that applied, such as HSBC and the Standard Chartered led group, faced uncertainty over launch timing, which affects product planning, partnerships and marketing spend tied to a stablecoin rollout.

Payment companies and crypto exchanges that want to use a regulated Hong Kong dollar stablecoin for settlement had to keep waiting, slowing plans to offer round-the-clock payments and tokenised asset trading.
Traditional businesses eyeing cheaper cross-border payments through stablecoins saw their adoption timelines pushed back, a reminder that being first in line still depends on the regulator's pace.
lawFirms: Fintech and financial regulatory teams advise applicants on meeting the Ordinance's reserve, governance and anti-money laundering requirements, work that intensifies while applications sit in review. The delay means more rounds of regulator engagement.
Banking and payments teams structure the partnerships and settlement arrangements that will run on these stablecoins once live, so they track licensing closely to time their clients' launches.
For candidates, this story shows how regulatory timing drives legal work in digital assets, a fast-growing practice where being able to explain the Stablecoins Ordinance is a genuine edge.

sources.title: Hong Kong hasn't issued a single HKD stablecoin license after March target (CoinDesk)

items.headline: Tax season goes fully digital: Hong Kong profits tax filing opens with mandatory e-filing for big firms
items.whatsTheNews: On 1 April 2026 Hong Kong's Inland Revenue Department issued its bulk profits tax returns for the 2025/26 year of assessment, kicking off the annual filing season. This year marks a shift toward compulsory electronic filing, with certain entities required to file through the new Business Tax Portal or a Tax Representative Portal rather than on paper. The IRD also issued large numbers of property tax and employer's returns the same day. Companies generally must file within one month of the issue date, with limited extensions for those who notified the IRD in advance. The move is part of Hong Kong's drive to modernise tax administration and align with global standards.
items.whatItMeans: Profits tax is the tax Hong Kong charges companies on their profits. A year of assessment is the tax year being reported on. A tax return is the form a business files to declare income and work out tax owed. E-filing means submitting that return online instead of on paper, and the IRD is now making this mandatory for many companies, especially larger groups. This matters because it changes how every business and its accountants handle compliance, and it ties into international reporting standards that big multinationals must follow.
clients: Large companies and multinational groups must upgrade systems and processes to file electronically, often in structured digital formats, which raises near-term compliance costs but speeds up filing over time.

Accounting firms and tax advisers face a busy season helping clients register for the new portals and meet tight filing windows, and they become essential to businesses unfamiliar with the digital process.
Smaller businesses still need to track issue dates and one-month deadlines carefully, since missing them can mean penalties, making the start of filing season a real operational pressure point.
lawFirms: Tax teams advise companies on filing obligations, the move to mandatory e-filing and how global minimum tax rules feed into Hong Kong returns. Filing season reliably generates advisory and compliance queries.

Corporate teams working on group structures and cross-border deals coordinate with tax colleagues, since digital filing and international reporting affect how transactions are documented. Candidates see tax and corporate work overlap here.
For trainees, understanding that Hong Kong is digitising tax and aligning with global standards is a concrete talking point about how regulation reshapes everyday client compliance work.

sources.title: 2025/26 profits tax filing season has commenced (EY Hong Kong Tax Alert)
A quick guide to the 2025/26 Hong Kong Profits Tax filing (KPMG Tax Alert Issue 6)

items.headline: Manycore Tech Clears HKEX Listing Hearing, Bringing Hong Kong IPO Pipeline to Boil
items.whatsTheNews: Manycore Tech, a Hangzhou-based artificial intelligence spatial design company, passed its HKEX listing hearing on 29 March 2026 and was preparing to launch its global offering to investors in the first week of April 2026. Manycore's main product, Kujiale, is an AI-powered cloud design platform used by interior designers and architects across China to create 3D spatial designs for residential and commercial renovation projects. The company is one of six high-growth technology companies from Hangzhou that became known as the 'Hangzhou Six Little Dragons', a cluster whose profile rose sharply in early 2025 following global attention given to DeepSeek, a Chinese AI company. Market participants were watching Manycore's offering as an early practical test of how HKEX's rules for specialist technology companies would work for an AI software business, and whether international institutional investors would pay premium valuations for Chinese AI-adjacent companies. The company targeted a listing on 17 April 2026 under stock code 00068 on HKEX's Main Board, with an indicative offer price of HK$6.72 to HK$7.62 per share.
items.whatItMeans: A listing hearing is the formal approval stage at HKEX at which the exchange's Listing Committee reviews a company's listing application and decides whether it meets all required standards. Passing the hearing is the final major regulatory hurdle before a company can open its shares to investors. An indicative price range is the band within which the final offer price will be set based on investor feedback during the pre-sale marketing process.
clients: Technology companies from mainland China evaluating a Hong Kong listing should monitor Manycore's offering closely, as it is an early practical test of how HKEX's Chapter 18C specialist technology listing rules work for AI software companies and whether they attract meaningful international institutional demand.

Institutional investors building exposure to Chinese AI companies through Hong Kong-listed securities should assess the concentration risk in Manycore's business, since Kujiale is primarily used in China's interior design sector at a time when China's residential property market is still recovering.

Cornerstone investors (strategic investors who commit before the IPO to buy and hold a fixed allocation for a minimum period) play a critical role in signalling market confidence, and their participation in Manycore's offering would be closely watched as a quality indicator by other institutional investors.
lawFirms: Sponsor law firms, which advise the investment banks managing the listing, must complete comprehensive due diligence on Manycore's intellectual property ownership, software licensing arrangements, data privacy practices, and compliance with China's data export and cybersecurity laws before the prospectus is filed.

Capital markets practices advising the company on listing readiness must ensure that any variable interest entity (VIE) structure used to allow foreign investment in the company is fully disclosed and explained in the prospectus in a way that satisfies HKEX's detailed disclosure standards.

Law firms advising underwriting banks must prepare underwriting agreements and indemnification provisions covering the accuracy of the prospectus, since underwriters bear significant legal exposure for material misstatements that cause investor losses after the listing.

sources.title: HK tech IPOs gain momentum as Manycore approaches listing (The Manila Times, 1 April 2026)
Manycore Tech announces Hong Kong IPO, first of Hangzhou Six Little Dragons to list (Global Times, 17 April 2026)

items.headline: Hong Kong's brokers had a record year: SFC says industry trading hit HK$219 trillion in 2025
items.whatsTheNews: On 31 March 2026 the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) published its Financial Review of the Securities Industry for the year ended 31 December 2025. The review showed earnings and trading activity at five-year highs for Hong Kong's securities firms. The total value of transactions handled by the industry jumped 52% year on year to HK$219.0 trillion, covering equities, bonds and other securities traded in Hong Kong and overseas. The surge reflects a much busier market in 2025, including a strong recovery in share trading and new listings. The SFC publishes this review each year to track the financial health of the brokers and firms it regulates.
items.whatItMeans: The SFC is Hong Kong's markets watchdog, regulating brokers, fund managers and other intermediaries. The Financial Review of the Securities Industry is an annual report card on how much money those firms made and how much trading flowed through them. Transaction value is the total dollar amount of trades executed, not profit. A jump to HK$219 trillion signals that confidence and volumes came roaring back, which matters because busy markets mean more fees, more hiring and more regulatory activity.
clients: Brokerages, investment banks and trading firms benefit directly from higher volumes through more commission and dealing income, which supports bonuses, hiring and expansion plans. A record year also tends to invite closer regulatory attention on conduct and controls.

Asset managers and exchange operators ride the same wave, since heavier trading lifts management fees and exchange revenue. Firms will use these numbers to justify investment in technology and headcount for the year ahead.

Companies thinking about listing or raising capital read this as a green light that demand and liquidity are strong, which can encourage more IPOs and follow-on raisings. That feeds the deal pipeline across the market.
lawFirms: Financial regulatory teams advise the same intermediaries on licensing, conduct rules and the internal controls that a booming, scrutinised market demands. More activity usually means more SFC inspections and enforcement, which keeps these teams busy.

Capital markets teams benefit because record trading and a recovering market support a fuller IPO and secondary fundraising pipeline. Junior lawyers see this in the volume of prospectus and listing work coming through.

Disputes and investigations teams should watch the flip side, as record activity often surfaces misconduct that the SFC later pursues. Candidates who track these industry numbers can speak credibly about why enforcement work tends to follow market booms.

sources.title: Earnings and transaction value surge to five-year highs for Hong Kong's securities industry in 2025

items.headline: HKMA bulletin takes stock of Hong Kong's bond market after a record 2025
items.whatsTheNews: On 31 March 2026 the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) published its first Bulletin of the year, Issue 01/2026. The headline feature is an article titled The Hong Kong Bond Market in 2025, reviewing how the city's debt market performed over the year. The Bulletin is the HKMA's regular research and policy publication, used to share analysis on banking, financial markets and payment systems. Bond market reviews like this track how much debt governments and companies raised in Hong Kong and who is buying it. The release sits alongside Hong Kong's broader push to grow as a fixed income and green bond hub.
items.whatItMeans: The HKMA is Hong Kong's central banking authority. It manages the currency peg, regulates banks and promotes financial stability. A bond is a loan that investors make to a government or company in return for interest, and the bond market is where that debt is issued and traded. The HKMA Bulletin is essentially the regulator's in-house journal. Tracking the bond market matters because deep, active debt markets let governments and businesses borrow cheaply, and Hong Kong wants to be the leading place in Asia to issue bonds, including green and tokenised ones.
clients: Banks and securities firms that arrange and underwrite bond issues read this to gauge demand and pricing trends, which shapes how aggressively they pitch new deals to corporate and government borrowers.

Corporate and sovereign issuers use the data to decide whether to raise money through Hong Kong bonds rather than loans or equity, and whether to tap green or offshore renminbi formats the city is promoting.
Institutional investors such as insurers and pension funds rely on a healthy bond market to put cash to work safely, so signals about depth and liquidity influence where they allocate.
lawFirms: Banking and finance and debt capital markets teams draft the prospectuses, trust deeds and subscription agreements behind bond issues, so a growing market means a steady flow of issuance mandates.

Teams building expertise in green, sustainability-linked and tokenised bonds are positioned for the formats Hong Kong is actively pushing, an area where the HKMA keeps innovating. Candidates can point to this as a fast-growing niche.

Regulatory lawyers advise issuers and arrangers on disclosure and listing requirements for debt, work that scales with the number of deals. Following HKMA publications is a simple way for a candidate to understand where this work comes from.

sources.title: HKMA Bulletin (Issue 01/2026) (HKMA press release)

items.headline: Hong Kong scraps paper share certificates: HKEX publishes rulebook for an all-electronic stock market
items.whatsTheNews: On 30 March 2026 the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong (HKEX) published the Listing Rule amendments that put its long-planned Uncertificated Securities Market (USM) into effect. USM means shares and other listed securities will be issued, held and transferred electronically instead of through paper share certificates. The Securities and Futures Commission confirmed the regime launches on 16 November 2026. Newly listed companies after that date must issue shares in uncertificated form from the start, and companies already listed will move across in phases over five years. The change brings Hong Kong into line with markets such as the UK, Australia and mainland China that already run paperless.
items.whatItMeans: A share certificate is a paper document proving you own shares. Uncertificated (or dematerialised) means ownership is recorded electronically with no paper at all. The Listing Rules are HKEX's rulebook that every listed company must follow. An Approved Securities Registrar is a regulated firm that keeps the electronic ownership records. The headline point is that investors will be able to hold shares directly in their own names electronically, rather than only through the central clearing system (CCASS), and companies have to update their constitutional documents and appoint a registrar to get ready.
clients: Every Hong Kong listed company will need to amend its articles of association to remove references to paper certificates and appoint an Approved Securities Registrar before its transition date. That is a compliance project with board approval and shareholder steps, so issuers cannot leave it to the last minute.

Share registrars, custodian banks and tech vendors stand to gain new mandates building and running the electronic registers, while the manual paper-handling business shrinks. Companies that depend on physical certificate processes face a cost and operations rethink.

Investors get faster, cheaper settlement and the option to be registered directly, which can change how companies communicate with shareholders and run votes. Issuers should plan investor relations around shareholders who now hold shares in their own names.
lawFirms: Capital markets and corporate teams pick up work advising issuers on the constitutional document changes, registrar appointments and disclosure needed to join USM. This is recurring, deadline-driven work across hundreds of listed companies, not a one-off.

Equity capital markets teams will fold USM compliance into every new IPO from November 2026, since fresh listings must be uncertificated from day one. Candidates should note this touches the standard listing process, so juniors will see it on most deals.

Funds and asset management lawyers advising REITs and SFC-authorised schemes that fall within USM scope will field client questions on timing and mechanics. Understanding USM is a concrete way for a trainee to show they follow Hong Kong market plumbing, not just headline deals.

sources.title: Uncertificated securities market regime – targeted for launch in November 2026 (Timothy Loh LLP)
HKEX Publishes Listing Rule Amendments to Implement Uncertificated Securities Market (Morgan Lewis)
